Iii . show the monarchical government to be the best form ; the first government that ever be ; agreeable to nature , wherein God found it ; consonant to God own government ; the most universal receive throughout the world ; the immediate and proper ordinance of God ; when the other government begin ; how allow by God ; the quality of elective kingdom , not primary the institution of God ; and the nature of the people . the monarchical government best . therefore it be apparent , that of all sort of government the monarchy be absolute the best , ( and of all monarch the best right be that which be hereditary ) because it be , 1. the first in nature . 2. the prime and principal ordinance of God . for , 1. reason . Selden in his title of honour , lib . 1. 2. though master Selden say , that natural all man in oeconomic rule , be equal free , and equal possess of superioty in those ancient propagation of mankind , even out of nature it self , and that inbred sociableness which every man have , as his character of civility , a popular state first raise it self , which by its own judgement afterward be convert into a monarchy ; and in the four page of his book reject the opinion of great philosopher , that affirm with saint Austin the first of the three government to be a monarchy , and affirm positive that the monarchy have its original out of a Democracy , as aristocracy likewise have ; yet i say , that this contradict his first thesis , where he assert that the husband , father , and master of the house rule as a king : and therefore the monarchy must needs be before either aristocracy or Democracy : and where cive Pausanias , that in boceticorum initio , say , nonlatinalphabet . monarchy ancient than any other government . all Greece be ancient under king and no democracy , he be drive to confess , page . 5. hat a family , be in nature before a public society or commonwealth , be a exemplary monarchy , and in that regard monarchy be to be acknowledge ancient than any other state ; and so not only the Orthodoxall people , but the pagan also have this notion thereof by the instinct of nature ; for the cappadocian be vanquish by the roman , monarchical government most agreeable to nature . do instant request they to give they a king , protest that they be not otherwise able to maintain themselves ; and so most other nation esteem that true , which herodian say , that as Jupiter have command over all the god , so in imitation of he , it be his pleasure , that the empire of man shall be monarchical . and indeed it be conclude by the common consent of the best philosopher that the law of nature lead we to a monarchy , monarchy found in nature . as when among all creature both animate and inanimate , we do always find one that have the pre-eminence above all the rest of his kind , as among the beast the lion , among fowl the eagle , among grain the wheat , among drink the wine , among spice the balm among metal the gold , among the element the fire , among the planet the sun ; and all the best divine conclude the monarchical government to be the most lively image and representation of the divine regiment and government of God , consonant to the divine government . who as sole monarch rule and guide all thing ; and therefore we find all the nation of great renown live under the royal government , as the scythian , aethiopian , Indians , assyrian , Mede , egyptian , bactrian , armenian , macedonian , Jew , and Romans first and last ; and at this day the most famous people live under this form , as the english , french , Spaniards , polonians , the government of the most famous nation monarchical . Danes , Muscovite , Tartar , Turk , abissines , moor , Agiamesques , zagathinians , cathaians ; yea and the savage people late discover in the west Indies , as be guide thereto only by the rule of nature , do all of they in a manner live under the government of king ; and i believe the apostle do special mean the regal government , — summo dull eius unum stare loco , s•••is•ue con e discordia regnis , Statius thebay . 1. though he speak plural of power , as understand the same of many king , because he speak but of one nonlatinalphabet , one sword , which be wrest out of the hand of the king , and put among many will make they all , like mad man , fall out and fight , which of they shall bear it , when one sword can never be well guide by many hand ; and therefore i think it be a madness indeed for any people to be weary of that government which God first ordain , which be most agreeable unto nature , most consonant to God government , most acceptable to God himself , and most profitable unto man , and to affect a late new invent government full of all danger and inconvenience . therefore it be apparent that monarchy be the first ordinance of all government ; a family be nothing else but a small kingdom , a family be a small kingdom , and a kingdom a great family . wherein the paterfamilias have regal power , and potestatem vita and necis , even over his own child , as i have elsewhere show in the example of Abraham , and of other heathens , that just execute their own son ; and a kingdom be nothing else but a great family , where the king have paternal power , and more than father now have , because of the great abuse that diverse father commit , while they have their plenary authority : therefore it be think fit to abridge they of that pristine power , and to place it all in the hand of the more public father . and to make this yet more plain unto the world , i will fain know of these democratical man , 1. when 2. how their Democracy and aristocracy have their be , and come first in use . i have show the age of monarchy to be from Adam . — prima { que } ab origine mundi , ad mea perpetuum deduxi tempora regem . and i can remember that any Democracie or aristocracy be in all the assyrian monarchy , when aristocracy and democracy begin . which notwithstanding last above a thousand year ; for the aristocracy of Greece , alas , they be but off yesterday , of no age , long after Homer time , which yet live but about the time of Jephte judge of Israel , and beside , i will not believe , — quicquid Graecia mendax audet in historjis — and for the Democracy of Rome , Titus Livius show when it be first hatch , after the expulsion of Tarquinius Superbus ; if therefore you will believe Tertullian , that id verius quod prius , you must needs give the precedency of all government unto monarchy . but that which be more considerable be to understand how these bird flit out of the nest of monarchy ? our saviour say , Matth. 15 . 13• . every plant which my father plant not shall be root up : that he plant monarchy , i have make it plain ; but when this vine begin to grow wild , what cause the change of monarchy . and instead of grape to bring forth bitter cluster , that be , oppression , instead of justice ; the people grow weary of God ordinance , and loath to be contain within the bound of obedience , when they find strength and opportunity they withstand their lawful but degenerate king , and then they depose they from their estate and deprive they of their life ; so that as the poet say ; Juvenai satyr . 10. ad generum cereris sine caede and sanguine pauci descendunt reges , and sicca morte tyranni . and think to find a better way than that which they find so thorny , and a better government than that which former they find so bad , they elect those man , who they think will make they happy , sometime more , the unconstancy of the people in the choice of their governor . and sometime few , as their disposition be , to be their governor : so after the expulsion of Tarquinius the Romans choose two consul , and these give not a plenary content unto the people , they add the tribune to bridle the disorder of the consul , and when all this will not satisfy their unsatiable expectation , they must have their decem viros , and in great dang•• their dictator , then come the triumvirat , of Antony , the government never settle till it come , as all thing in nature , nonlatinalphabet , to monarchy . Lepidus , and Augustus , who at last take upon he the name of a emperor , but the full power of a king , and govern all as the sole monarch : thus they run in a ma•e , and turn round like a wheel : and i shall but weary my reader to trace the greek history , to set down the state of Athens , under the thirty tyrant , or of the lacedem•nians under those Eph•r•• . that bear a fair show to restrain their king , lacedaemoniorumaristocratia ex dvobus regibusquinque Ephori• , octo and viginti senatoribus composita• but be indeed a scourge and plague unto the people ; so that in truth , the remedy prove far worse than the disease , excessit medicina modum , and the change of government never bring any other good , but a exchange of misery , the great for the lesser , unto the people , as for that one rape of Lucrece by Tarquinius , to undergo a thousand great insolence under the new erect government of the consul and tribune ; and the Israelites for prevent the snatch of the flesh out of their pot , 1 Sam 2.14.15 by the son of Eli , and grow weary of the son of Samuel , Chap. 8.11 . to have a Saul , that shall tear their own flesh in piece , and take their son and their daughter for his vassal . 2. reason that monarchy be the best form of government . 2. as the hereditary monarch be the first kind of government , so it be the principal and best government ; because it be the immediate ordinance of God , that he set down for the government of his people ; for this be ordain by God himself , and so continue among his people , even in a hereditary way , unless the same God design another person by those prophet , that he inspire for that purpose ; as it be in the case of David , Solomon , and Jehu ; and it be certain , that the wise of man can devise a better form of government than God ordain : therefore the choice of one or more , make by the people to be their king or governor , can be ( if not without sin ) yet i be sure , without folly ; but see , as our saviour say , a sparrow can light upon the ground without the providence of our heavenly father : Matth. 10 : 29 : so i must confess ; — hac non sine numine divûm eveniunt — this election of king and change of the first ordina••e happen not without God providence , either for the tyranny of the evil king , or the punishment of the rebellious people : and therefore as Moses for the hardness of those man heart that hate their wife , to prevent a great mischief , either continual fight , or secret murder one another , suffer they to give their wife a bill of divorcement , Deut. 24.1 , ( but as our saviour say , non erat sic ab initio , Matth. 19.8 . it be not any primary ordinance of God , but a permissive toleration of the lesser evil ; so when the people out of their froward disposition to God first institution of the regal right , and presume to like better of their own choice do alter this hereditary right and divine ordinance into the election of one or more governor , how God allow the aristocratical and democratical government , and why . either annual , as among the ancient Romans , or vital , as it be in the present state of the venetian , ; God , out of his infinite lenity to our humane frailty , rather than he people shall be without government , and so many heinous sin shall go unpunished do permit , and it may be allow and approve the same , though sometime not without great anger and indignation for our contempt and distaste of his heavenly institution ; that be , vers . 7. they have refuse to submit themselves to his ordinance , and to obey the king that he appoint over they , but they must needs be their own carver and have a king of their own election , or such a king ( invest with a more absolute power ) as they desire , though notwithstanding they do most hypocritical seem to desire none but who God appoint over they ; it be requisite that we shall consider those thing that be most necessary to a christian king , for the religious performance of this duty : and they be three thing necessary for a king to preserve the church and true religion . 1. a will to perform it . 2. a understand to go about it . 3. a power to effect it . and these three must be inseparable in the prince that maintain true religion . for 1. our knowledge and our power without a willing mind do want motion . 2. our will and power without knowledge shall never be able to move right . and 3. our will and knowledge without ability can never prevail to produce any effect . therefore king and prince ought to labour to be furnish with these three special grace . the first be a good will to preserve the purity of God service , 1. a willing mind to do it . not only in his house , but also throughout all his kingdom : and this , as all other grace be , must be acquire by our faithful prayer , and that in a more special manner for king and prince then for any other ; John . 15. 5. — without i you can do nothing . the right meaning and understand of these word of Christ give light to the truth in these treatise , which must needs be include in one of these six expression follow . first , that Christ be the helper of man in the good work . or second , that Christ be a cooperator with man in the work . or three , that Christ be the instrument of man in the work . or fourthly , that man be the helper of Christ in the work . or five , that man be the cooperator with Christ in the work . or sixthly , that man be the instrument only of Christ in the work . Solut. if Christ be the helper of man in the work , then be man the beginner and master of the work , the principal worker or workman of the work , and man be exalt above Christ . if Christ be a cooperator with man , than Christ be only a partner with man in the work , and so Christ have only his share with man of the work , Christ be disthron . if Christ be the instrument of man in the work , than Christ be not the doer of the work , but man be the doer ; the work be whole man : for the instrument be only fit , guide , and act by the workman ; and so be Christ only fit , guide , and act by man , if Christ be his instrument : man be exalt above Christ . if man be the helper of Christ in the good work , than Christ be not able and strong enough , or not sufficient to do and perform the good deed , but have need of man help : so man be exalt , which 2 Cor. 12 11 Gal. 6. 3 be nothing , ( as the apostle say . ) if man be a cooperator with Christ in the good work , than man may of right claim a share in the work , and of the work that be righteous ; and so man have something to boast of , contrary to the apostle , 1 Cor. 9 16 and man be exalt . if man be only ( by grace ) a fit instrument of Christ in his good and righteous work ( as he please , ) than Christ be the only doer and workman of the work , and the work be whole Christ ; the honour whole belong to he : and man be so make gracious his instrument also of the praise and glory of his grace ; especial for Christ account and impute his own work , and the righteousness thereof , to man ; as Paul say David say , bless be the man to whomthe lord impute righteousness without work of his own .
